step_by_step_thinking:
I agree with the assessment that this presentation strongly suggests mucormycosis. One additional piece of supporting evidence could be the presence of black necrotic tissue often seen in cases of mucormycosis, which can further differentiate it from other infections like cellulitis where such findings may not typically occur.

```plaintext

I concur with the diagnosis of mucormycosis given the hard swelling and multiple draining sinuses indicative of an aggressive fungal infection. Additionally, the location at the angle of the mandible aligns well with reported sites commonly affected by mucormycosis, particularly if there has been any underlying predisposing factor such as diabetes mellitus or immunosuppression.
